text: This is a case of a patient requiring a laparoscopic splenectomy for splenomegaly. The CT scan shows an enlarged spleen. The spleen measured approximately seventeen point four centimeters in maximal dimensions. It has been growing over time.
The surgeon places a 15mm port in the left abdomen, preparing for the laparoscopic splenectomy procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, identifies the inferior pole of the spleen, and mobilizes the splenic flexure of the colon along with the left colon. The spleen is reflected anteriorly, and attachments to the kidney, colon, and spleen are mobilized. The tail of the pancreas is visible posterior to the grasper as the spleen is flipped medially.
The surgeon inserts an additional 5mm port into the left flank to retract the spleen laterally, creating space to isolate the vessels at the splenic hilum.

The surgeon dissects along the superior edge of the pancreas, revealing the splenic artery. The lesser sac is entered, facilitating the mobilization of the short gastric vessels up to the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon continues mobilizing the short gastric vessels, nearly completing their dissection. The posterior attachments of the spleen and the superior pole are being carefully mobilized, with efforts to completely isolate the splenic vessels visible.
The surgeon has completely isolated the inflow pedicle, which includes both the splenic vein and splenic artery. A vascular stapler is prepared to transect these vessels.
The surgeon prepares for a second firing of the vascular stapler on the isolated splenic vessels, with a grasper positioned nearby to control any potential bleeding.

The surgeon performs a second firing of the vascular stapler, achieving complete control over the inflow and outflow from the spleen. Additional posterior and superior attachments are being mobilized to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon isolates and removes the final attachments of the spleen, achieving excellent hemostasis. A large specimen retrieval bag is used through a fifty millimeter port to contain the spleen, which is then morselized and extracted through a fifteen millimeter port site.
